title: Create the Gapminder dataset
text:
# Import necessary libraries
import pandas as pd
from gapminder import gapminder

# Rename 'lifeExp' to 'life_expectancy' and 'gdpPercap' to 'gdp_per_capita'
gapminder = gapminder.rename(columns={'lifeExp': 'life_expectancy',
                                      'pop': 'population_millions', 
                                      'gdpPercap': 'gdp_per_capita'})

# Convert population to millions
gapminder['population_millions'] = gapminder['population_millions'] / 1_000_000

# Create a new pandas DataFrame from the modified gapminder data
gapminder_df = pd.DataFrame(gapminder)

# Save the DataFrame as a CSV file
gapminder_df.to_csv('gapminder.csv', index=False)

# Print the first 3 rows of the DataFrame
print(gapminder_df.head(3))

title: Life Expectancy and GDP per Capita
text:
This graph shows the relationship between GDP per capita and life expectancy for 142 countries from 1952 to 2007. Each point represents a country-year observation, and colors indicate continents. The x-axis is displayed on a logarithmic scale to better visualize income differences across countries.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("gapminder.csv")

# A simple global view: scatter of all country-year points, colored by continent
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for cont, sub in df.groupby("continent"):
    ax.scatter(sub["gdp_per_capita"], sub["life_expectancy"], label=cont, alpha=0.6)

ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("GDP per capita (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("Life expectancy")
ax.set_title("Life Expectancy vs GDP per Capita (All Countries, 1952–2007)")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

title: Life Expectancy and GDP per Capita
text:
This graph shows the relationship between GDP per capita and life expectancy for 142 countries from 1952 to 2007. Each point represents a country-year observation, and colors indicate continents. The x-axis is displayed on a logarithmic scale to better visualize income differences across countries.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("gapminder.csv")

# A simple global view: scatter of all country-year points, colored by continent
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for cont, sub in df.groupby("continent"):
    ax.scatter(sub["gdp_per_capita"], sub["life_expectancy"], label=cont, alpha=0.6)

ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("GDP per capita (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("Life expectancy")
ax.set_title("Life Expectancy vs GDP per Capita (All Countries, 1952–2007)")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

title: Life Expectancy and GDP per Capita
text:
Interpretation
There is a strong positive relationship between GDP per capita and life expectancy. Countries with higher income levels generally have longer life expectancy. However, the increase in life expectancy slows at higher income levels, suggesting diminishing returns to income. Clear continental patterns are also visible, with Europe and Oceania clustered at higher income and life expectancy levels, while many African countries remain at lower levels.

title: Country Analysis
text:
This page tracks changes in life expectancy, GDP per capita, and population over time for one country.
#| echo: true import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
df = pd.read_csv(“gapminder.csv”)
country_name = “China” c = df[df[“country”] == country_name].sort_values(“year”)
fig, ax = plt.subplots() ax.plot(c[“year”], c[“life_expectancy”], marker=“o”) ax.set_xlabel(“Year”) ax.set_ylabel(“Life expectancy”) ax.set_title(f”{country_name}: Life Expectancy Over Time”) plt.show()
fig, ax = plt.subplots() ax.plot(c[“year”], c[“gdp_per_capita”], marker=“o”) ax.set_xlabel(“Year”) ax.set_ylabel(“GDP per capita”) ax.set_title(f”{country_name}: GDP per Capita Over Time”) plt.show()
fig, ax = plt.subplots() ax.plot(c[“year”], c[“population_millions”], marker=“o”) ax.set_xlabel(“Year”) ax.set_ylabel(“Population (millions)”) ax.set_title(f”{country_name}: Population Over Time”) plt.show()

title: Gapminder: Life, Wealth, and Population
text: Gapminder is a dataset tracking life expectancy, population, and GDP per capita for 142 countries from 1952 to 2007 (every 5 years).

title: Life Expectancy and Population
text:
This graph examines the relationship between population size and life expectancy across countries in 2007. Each point represents a country, and colors indicate continents. Population is shown on a logarithmic scale to account for large differences between countries.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("gapminder.csv")

# Use latest year to avoid repeated country points across time
latest_year = df["year"].max()
latest = df[df["year"] == latest_year].copy()

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for cont, sub in latest.groupby("continent"):
    ax.scatter(sub["population_millions"], sub["life_expectancy"], label=cont, alpha=0.7)

ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Population (millions, log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("Life expectancy")
ax.set_title(f"Life Expectancy vs Population by Continent ({latest_year})")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

title: Life Expectancy and Population
text:
This graph examines the relationship between population size and life expectancy across countries in 2007. Each point represents a country, and colors indicate continents. Population is shown on a logarithmic scale to account for large differences between countries.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("gapminder.csv")

# Use latest year to avoid repeated country points across time
latest_year = df["year"].max()
latest = df[df["year"] == latest_year].copy()

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for cont, sub in latest.groupby("continent"):
    ax.scatter(sub["population_millions"], sub["life_expectancy"], label=cont, alpha=0.7)

ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Population (millions, log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("Life expectancy")
ax.set_title(f"Life Expectancy vs Population by Continent ({latest_year})")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

title: Life Expectancy and Population
text:
Interpretation
Unlike GDP per capita, population size does not show a strong relationship with life expectancy. Both small and large countries can have high or low life expectancy. This suggests that population size alone is not a key determinant of health outcomes. Instead, economic development and other institutional factors likely play a more important role.
